ac:rich-text-body.p: The SKA Sensitivity Calculator is an online tool for estimating observational sensitivity and planning SKA observations. It computes the noise level, beam properties, and required integration time for continuum and spectral-line observations with SKA-Mid and SKA-Low.

p: The Sensitivity Calculator helps astronomers plan SKA observations by predicting the sensitivity achievable under specific observing conditions. You can:

ul.li: Calculate the minimum detectable flux density (sensitivity in Jy) for a given integration time
Estimate the integration time needed to reach a target sensitivity level
Predict beam sizes and shape at your desired frequency
Assess surface brightness sensitivity and confusion limits
Optimise array configuration choices (frequency band, bandwidth, etc.)

p: Results account for real receiver parameters, baseline distributions, atmospheric effects, and RFI mitigation strategies for each Array Assembly stage of SKA-Mid and SKA-Low.
The calculator supports both SKA telescope configurations:

ul.li: SKA-Mid: Mid-frequency array (250 MHz – 14 GHz) composed of 197 13.5-metre dishes in South Africa
SKA-Low: Low-frequency array (50 – 350 MHz) composed of ~130,000 log-periodic antennas in Australia

p: Both telescopes are available in all Array Assembly stages (AA1, AA2, AA*, AA4) to match your proposal timeline.
Wideband continuum mode with full bandwidth for continuum surveys, extragalactic science, and pulsar timing. The calculator uses standard receiver bandwidths for each frequency band.

High-resolution spectral-line mode with zoom capability. Allows selection of narrower sub-bands within the receiver passband for targeted spectroscopy, HI surveys, or molecular line studies. Spectral resolution is selectable in steps typical of correlator configuration.
When using the calculator, you specify:

tr.td: AA1, AA2, AA*, or AA4 (determines number of active elements)
Centre frequency of observation (MHz or GHz)
For continuum, receiver bandwidth; for zoom, selected sub-band width
Right Ascension and Declination (for parallactic angle and elevation corrections)

ul.li: Sensitivity (Jy): 1-sigma thermal noise in image plane
Beam Size: Full-width half-maximum (major and minor axes) in arcseconds
Beam Position Angle: Orientation on the sky
Surface Brightness Sensitivity: Sensitivity to extended emission (mJy/beam)
Confusion Noise: Brightness fluctuation limit from unresolved sources
Integration Time: (If target sensitivity was specified instead)

ac:rich-text-body.p: The calculator tool is embedded below. Enter your parameters and click "Calculate" to see results in real time. Hover over parameter labels for detailed explanations.

h2: Using the Calculator for Proposal Planning

ac:rich-text-body.p: To estimate time needed for a survey: select your target frequency, desired sensitivity, field size, and array stage. The calculator will show required integration time. Remember to factor in slew time, calibration overheads, and observing efficiency (~80%).
Try the same integration time at different frequencies to see how sensitivity scales with frequency and how beam size improves at higher frequencies. This helps prioritise science objectives.
Compare sensitivity at different Array Assembly stages (AA1 vs AA*) to understand when your science becomes feasible, and to decide whether to split observations across deployment phases.

p: For scripting, batch calculations, or offline use, the full SKA Sensitivity Calculator is available as a standalone web service at:
This standalone instance provides the same calculation engine with additional options for advanced users and programmatic access.
The Sensitivity Calculator is built on open-source software released under the BSD-3-Clause License. The source code is available on the SKA Observatory GitLab repository:
You can fork the code to customise calculations for your own studies or contribute improvements back to the project.

ul.li: Calculations assume ideal receivers with specified noise temperatures (may be optimistic for early Array Assembly stages)
RFI environment is assumed to be as designed (actual RFI may reduce sensitivity at some frequencies)
Confusion limits are estimated from source counts; actual values depend on source spectral properties
Beam sidelobe effects are not detailed; see the full technical documentation for sidelobe predictions
Correlator channel count is rounded to standard configurations; non-standard setups require proposal discussion
